category.name: zoo.window.shopAnimalInfo
category.item: These cheerful parrots are very chatty! Always keep them as a pair or group. Visitors love these smart guys with their brightly colored feathers!
Don't you still have these little urchins in your zoo?! Everyone loves these restless creatures who are always up to mischief. A zoo without meerkats must be pretty boring...
A small furry animal that is always on the move! These possums run around the enclosure, clean themselves, crawl into every corner and entertain the audience.
These beautiful pink animals attract the attention of visitors. Their main food is plankton, but they also enjoy fresh fish. What a pretty beak they have!

Stripes, stripes, stripes everywhere! It's mind-boggling! In a world full of zebra stripes, it's good camouflage, but in our part of the world, zebras look quite comical. Zebras definitely belong in every zoo, so don't let that fool you!
In the wild, these wood experts are hard to spot. But at the zoo, anyone can watch these nimble builders with large incisors at work.
In the water they are fast as a bullet and elegant. Their prey has no chance. But on land they run around clumsily and attract attention and laughter. But they always look good in a tailcoat.
Finally someone who likes to jump big! Kangaroos really do have that in their blood. But be careful! If you get too close to their pouch, you might get a sharp blow from their tail.
These large pachyderms are loners. It's no wonder they wear such horns! Despite their powerful appearance, they rarely engage in fights. However, it's best to keep only a male and a female together.
Chimpanzees are so playful. The attendance at the chimpanzee enclosure is huge. These funny monkeys are always on the move and provide entertainment.
The king of beasts, who wears a majestically beautiful mane instead of a crown. But that's a real mane! Be careful! This is not an animal to be petted!

On land they look like lumbering colossuses, but appearances are deceiving. Be careful not to anger them, as once they start running, it is not advisable to get in their way. Fortunately, they are easy-going animals if their privacy is respected.

Always high in the clouds! Giraffes reach for the sweetest fruits in the treetops. So don't be surprised if they literally graze the trees. With their long necks, they reach very high and prefer to eat green leaves!
These peaceful pachyderms like to live in herds. Take good care of them and they will surely repay you! And remember, an elephant never forgets when someone makes them angry!
A polar bear can also hit hard with its furry paws. But it prefers to roll around on its ice floe. It is also a great fisherman, even without a fishing rod!
Pandas are the main attraction of any zoo. Everyone admires these bushy, exotic animals with black and white fur as they cuddle and sit in the grass or chew on bamboo branches.
It's not every day that you get to see such an impressive silverback up close. The gorilla enclosure is constantly surrounded by curious visitors. However, they are also a big attraction at any zoo.
Hooray for the petting zoo! Here you can pet every animal! But those cute bunnies are not as innocent as everyone thinks. They breed like a race and will eat everything around them!
Don't worry! There's no rush... Visitors are fascinated by these furry companions - and maybe even a little jealous. Sloths rest until it hurts!
What? You only know crocodiles as handbags? Well, it's time to take a trip to the zoo! But don't get too close to them. Once they bite, they won't let go!
Giant tortoises are known for their longevity. Some live to be over 100 years old. Visitors admire their large shells. Their motto: Keep calm and hide your head in case of trouble!

How unique! Lemurs belong to the suborder "wet-nosed monkeys" and live mainly in trees, where they can see nothing with their round eyes. However, they like to close their eyes during the day and are active again at night.
In the North American prairies and forests, you have to be careful not to scare the bison herds. The American bison is a truly exotic animal that even survived the Ice Age.

A gray meerkat with a head full of mischief. He loves golf and is always up to something. In the process, he often forgets what is important in life. It's good that you take care of his water and food. Only available for a short time!
Bats are shy and active at night. They navigate their caves using sound waves. They are rarely seen in the wild, but even here at the zoo you can admire their sophisticated hunting technique!
These creepy bloodsuckers are the vampires of animals. They avoid the light of day and only go on the rampage at night. Uuuuaaaahhh! Available for a short time only!
In the South American Andes, the llama is a popular draft animal. It looks cute, but don't upset it, it might spit on you.
You just have to love koala bears. They are cute, climb trees and enjoy eating eucalyptus leaves, which zoo visitors enjoy watching.

These big cats are the main attraction for many zoo visitors. They move around their enclosure on their velvet paws with agility and majesty, and occasionally show off their powerful jaws. They command respect!
A truly royal animal with snow-white fur that is rarely seen in the wild! From a distance, it looks like a cuddly kitten, but this beautiful white tiger is a very dangerous beast.

Packs of wolves prowl the forests in pursuit of prey that stands no chance against the tactics of these animals. And their howling at the full moon awakens a strange feeling of fear not only among chickens and sheep. That is why there are many legends about them.
Far in the north, they live in herds of reindeer. Even in harsh Arctic conditions, they find a place to survive.
Here we go again: Rudolph the Reindeer with a red, frosty nose that glows in the dark. Everyone loves Rudolph, so he'll be stopping by again. Hurry, buy him!
A panther creeps silently through its territory: Only its bright eyes shine in the darkness. Visitors admire the gleaming black fur of this noble and rare beast.
Due to its color, this antelope is also called the white oryx. It is perfectly adapted to life in the desert. Aren't its horns impressive? It will become a real gem of your zoo.
Oh...\n a little fear, these four-legged mute faces are more than a little scary. They are beasts after all. They stay mainly on the ground and feel strongest in a group. Watch out for them..Vrrr...

A truly unique animal. So graceful, so majestic. In Thailand, the white elephant is even considered sacred. In your zoo, it will be a true treasure that you must take the best care of. How beautifully it trumpets: Tra-tra!
The black zebra is a small exotic. It is still striped, but it surprises with a significant proportion of black. Visitors will not see such an animal every day. Have your cameras ready!

This type of antelope is also called the "black heel". This is because a tuft of hair grows above their hoof on each hind leg. It looks funny. They live in the savannahs, where they move with quick jumps. Hop, Hop, hop!

Oh... how breathtaking it is when this predator spreads its wings. It likes areas with plenty of water where it can quickly reach its favorite food - fish. And check out its huge yellow beak - it's a real treat!

Grr, grr, grizzly. It owes its name to its shaggy fur, which has a grayish tinge. The English word "grizzle" means gray. Every visitor is taken aback when it stands on its hind legs. Visitors even stand in a long line to see the grizzly.
The largest bird in the world, it has a very hard time flying. Instead, it runs back and forth across savannah and desert areas, unstoppable.
Better a white dove in your hand than a sparrow on the roof of a pet store. The shining feathers make this elegant bird a real eye-catcher. When pigeons flock together, visitors flock to see them.
Love gives us wings, and the love dove is just the thing for the zoo director's romantic heart. But you'll have to act fast: it's only available for a short time.

The peacock is a symbol of beauty. When the male spreads his feathers, everyone stands in awe. The male can proudly show off in front of his peahen. Gossip, gossip: Isn't a baby peacock hatching from this egg?
Hop, bunny! With her white fur, Alice the bunny is a real speciality. She brings great joy to visitors! But this curious animal is only available for a short time!
He is truly the king of scorpions and can defend himself well with his scissors. But don't worry, he's just playing...
An armadillo does have a belt, but not around its waist. Instead, it has a hard shell with movable belts on its back. Small armadillos also like to hide in bushes. And look at that long tongue!
The masked agama is truly breathtaking when it suddenly spreads its beautiful collar. However, it only shows it when it feels threatened; otherwise, it is shy and peaceful and would not harm anyone.
A real fox cannot be missing from a zoo. It is a great attraction for visitors. And if there is too much noise around it, it will disappear into its shelter. Just as cunning as ... a fox.
This monkey is slender and agile. It can swing from tree to tree with its long arms - always looking for tasty fruits or leaves.
A real rarity in the zoo and a pretty heavy weight to boot. On the scales at the zoo, you can see that a rhinoceros weighs up to 2 tons. That's a nice portion of fruit and leaves.
Look into my eyes, Helenka! The cross-eyed possum is world famous - he is undoubtedly the star of the enclosure. Helenka, how sweetly you squint!
These scaly creatures have several interesting tricks: With their rubber tongue, they can reliably catch flies in flight and also change their color at will.
When a raccoon holds food in its hands, it looks as if it is washing it. That is why it has such a funny name. The little forest dweller has just washed himself.
It's nice to be in this world, says the porcupine bee. But if the porcupine straightens its quills, visitors better beware!
Alligators belong to the crocodilian family. They are a bit calmer than crocodiles themselves. But that doesn't mean they can't attack unexpectedly. Their favorite place is a mudflat in the sun.
Phew! Can you smell it too? Our little stinker is spraying a foul-smelling liquid as a warning. Get out of his way, you won't be able to wash this off easily!
The largest of the deer family - and a real crowd magnet. Its large antlers deserve respect. Too bad you can't just touch them... so they're soft after all?
Two things are important to a wild boar - shelter and water! They simply cannot do without them. Oh ... and they also like to roll in the mud.
That's something! The second largest land animal on Earth loves evergreen forests and is pretty cunning at it! Look closely: The tip of its trunk is used as a finger. How practical!
What are those sounds? It's a rattlesnake! It's a rather shy creature. But be careful! Anyone who gets close to it can get bitten. Besides, it's a poisonous reptile.
The lynx has excellent night vision. It owes it to its brilliant eyes that shine into the dark night. Isn't that amazing?
How cute, the little hopscotch is here! Visitors would love to cuddle with it for hours.
Your visitors are delighted with the huge toucan nose. But these birds are not so interested in it. They would rather enjoy the juicy fruits.

Don't be fooled! The snow leopard looks like a cuddly cat, but it's a wild beast! The long tail not only looks chic, but also protects against the cold. They simply wrap themselves in it and take a nap.
No one has actually seen this creature before. The Great Footprint is very, very shy, so most people think it's extinct. Its footprints are often faked, prove it really exists!

The smallest representative of the fox family is not only very cute, but also shy. You will rarely meet it face to face. It hunts at night and if disturbed, it will bury itself in the sand as quickly as possible.
Flying squirrels? Yes, they exist! These little rodents jump from tree to tree by spreading their skin between their limbs. They can jump up to 50 meters. Isn't that great?
Nobility and purity - these are the characteristics of the majestic swan. Its beauty and courtship dances are admired by everyone. Swan chicks are often ugly, but they grow up to be beautiful swans.

The famous "ships of the desert" can store a lot of fat and water in their humps. It's no wonder they're often used for trips into the desert. But a camel ride like that can make you feel pretty sick.

Srrrk! Walruses use their long tusks to dig up shells from the seabed, which they then simply suck up. To be sufficiently full, they have to eat up to 3,000-6,000 of them. But don't worry, they also like fish and don't need that many.
This cat ghost is tiptoeing through Halloween night. See those cute green eyes? They let him see you in the dark.

These rare and special little creatures are looking for a new home. Red Pandas (also known as Little Pandas) may be smaller than their black and white relatives, but they can easily put them in your pocket when it comes to cuteness.

The Adamicus penguin, or Adam, is the smartest of the birds in tailcoats. Scientists are still debating what led to some of this "Super Penguin" species even speaking. Is it a whim of nature or is there something more behind it?
A true giant among lizards: These scaly lizards grow to be up to 3 m long and weigh 70 kg. A huge attraction for your zoo!
Tapirs love to roll in the mud - but these clumsy fatties have nothing in common with pigs, which would be like comparing horses to rhinoceroses.
To keep the snowy owl's feet from freezing in the winter, Mother Nature gave it fluffy, warm socks made of feathers. These also keep this plump bird from getting stuck in the snow.
Come into my arms! The calm apes with red, bushy fur have two exceptionally long arms with which they can wonderfully hug young orangutans.
One little moan and the shy doe disappears into the forest undergrowth with one or two graceful leaps. Her cute spotted offspring will now delight your visitors with its big deer eyes.
You'll be amazed! With their large ears and unusual coloring, these rainforest animals are among the most striking inhabitants of your zoo. Aren't they beautiful?
To get to his favorite food - Honey, the honey-eater lets his little bird helper, the Honey-Whisperer, show him the way to the hive. How practical!
Hello, blondie! It's truly a sight to behold when the silky mane of the Golden Lions shines in the sun.
Meeee! That's a cute lamb! This ball of wool loves to snuggle up to his mom, but when it's time to go to pasture, he likes to frolic in the grass with the other sheep.
The king of beasts, who wears a majestically beautiful mane instead of a crown. But that's a real mane! Be careful! This is not an animal to be petted!
The king of beasts, who wears a majestically beautiful mane instead of a crown. But that's a real mane! Be careful! This is not an animal to be petted!

This species of kangaroo has several winning trophies: Not only is it the largest representative of the kangaroos, but it is also the largest living marsupial. In addition, it also has the potential to become the next world boxing champion. A real winner for the zoo!
Big dark eyes, smooth fur - the grey kangaroo looks very cute. But don't let that fool you! Like all kangaroos, the grey kangaroo can really give it a go!
These strange animals sleep all day and are only active at night. Perhaps they frolic at parties when the zookeeper isn't looking?
Frogs of the genus Frog King are very special. They sit majestically in their enclosures and let themselves be admired by zoo visitors. Will they turn into a prince or princess after a kiss?
Despite their impressive appearance, rhinos are calm and shy animals. But that changes if they feel threatened - once they get moving, they become an unstoppable avalanche!
The largest member of the marsupial family is a real devil! Energetic, loud, not at all shy - this devil from Tanzania is an exciting addition to any zoo! He just doesn't like being petted very much.
Mammoths are among the most famous animals from the Ice Age. No wonder! Their huge bodies and tusks are truly impressive! Not only visitors but also elephants are amazed by their hunchback!
The white rhinoceros loves to chat with other rhinos. Especially the males are real talkers! Get these chatterboxes for your zoo!
The two-horned rhinoceros has very poor vision, just like its rhinoceros brethren. Whether your visitors notice this is another matter... they might need glasses too!
The Australian crocodile is a very shy reptile and prefers to hide, but anyone who gets close to it will pay with their life.
The white crocodile has no chance of surviving in the wild because it cannot blend in with its surroundings. However, it is a curiosity for every zoo and attracts many visitors.
This powerful, armored reptile lies motionless in the water. Is it sleeping? Or is it contemplating life? No, it is definitely contemplating its next meal.
Don't confuse it with the parrot that shares the same name! The Lori is a small, fluffy-furred prosimian.
With elegance, the cobra silently crawls through the tall grass, looking for something to eat. What a shame that such a beautiful animal is so dangerous!
This strange animal lives in Africa and loves termites the most! The groundhog is a nocturnal creature and also enjoys eating cucumbers.
Yay! The donkey stubbornly refuses to move and lies motionless on the ground. How about luring him with a carrot!
The eagle adorns many emblems and coats of arms. No wonder! Look how majestically it soars in the air: The true king of the sky!
With a body length of almost one meter, this eagle is the largest of its kind. Give this endangered raptor a cozy home - even your visitors will admire this graceful animal.
A good portion: The female can also weigh up to 9 kg. It's an incredible spectacle when the largest of predators gracefully soars in the air.
Warm feet: After hatching, penguin chicks stay snuggled on their parents' feet for another 9 months.
As their name suggests, king penguins have a bright plume of orange-yellow feathers on their heads. You can find out for yourself at your local zoo whether this helps them swim faster.
Since when do penguins like winter? The Humboldt penguin lives on the coast of Peru and likes the sun shining on its white belly.
As one of the largest felines, this yellow-and-black spotted jaguar is anything but a friendly kitty. Don't even touch them!
Such a little thing! This little bird feeds on nectar from flowers. Its fluttering wings are usually only visible under a magnifying glass, and their rapid fluttering resembles that of an insect.
The Arctic wolf has a snow coat, which optimally camouflages it in the snow. Living in a pack is necessary for survival in this harsh environment.
At birth, the timber wolf is mostly black. With each molt, the color becomes lighter. It lives in a strict hierarchical pack, where the alpha wolf is the leader.
This species of wolf is rare and almost extinct. It is slightly thinner and smaller than other wolf species. Its fur is yellowish-brown or reddish.
This fun companion looks like a beaver, has a beak like a duck, and runs like a lizard. Plus, this cute little animal has a poisonous spine, so it's not allowed to be petted!
The anteater uses its long, sticky tongue to search for tiny, tasty ants, which is why it needs such a long snout.
This lizard lives mostly in tall trees, where its green skin blends in best with its surroundings. It is a slow, diurnal animal.
The Black Angora rabbit is known for its long fur, which needs to be brushed regularly. Angora rabbits love it!
These sweet-eared dogs are very tame and gentle. Maybe it's because they have long ears that affect their hearing and they're not easily startled?
These rabbits have long manes and resemble lion cubs. They also look very cute and a little cheeky.
The guanaco is very hairy, which is why it is often hunted. It's a good thing they are not extinct yet.
These white llamas are truly rare. With their split upper lips, they stuff themselves with grass and herbs.
Like all llamas, the alpaca has long, narrow legs and a slender neck. Their fluffy, soft wool makes you want to snuggle up to it.
With its red and blue coloration on its face and back, the mandrill is the most colorful primate. After hominids, mandrills are the largest primates.
Black as night, the raven flies around the trees and fields on foggy, autumn nights. Sometimes it catches a pumpkin. Terrible!
Spider! The venomous bite of this crawling creature can result in unpleasant side effects, such as a wild dance. This type of dance is known as a 'tarantula' and its name is derived from this spider.
The red-billed toucan is found at altitudes above 1400 meters, with the forests of the Andes in South America being its preferred home.
The red-bellied toucan flaunts its large blue-green beak and red belly in the forests of South America.
The white-faced toucan is a very large bird at 48 cm. Its yellow neck contrasts brightly with its black coat.
Puffins get a lot of attention with their funny beaks. They look a bit like clowns and can be found from the Atlantic Ocean to the polar regions.

This medium-sized anteater is found in the southern part of the Andes Mountains. It likes to feast on insects and ants and is also good at defending itself - it will stand on its hind legs, lean against a tree and swing forward with its sharp claws!
This anteater is found in the northern part of the Andes. It is a solitary animal that crawls on the ground, bending its claws. It also likes to swing from branch to branch sometimes, yuck!
This little guy is the only anteater that lives in trees. He doesn't have a long snout like other anteaters, but he loves ants or bugs.
Squirrels are especially active in the fall when they gather food for the winter. Look how cute they are with nuts in their paws!
The Arctic fox has brown-beige fur in the summer and white in the winter. They blend in perfectly with the snow.
The white peacock, also known as the bridal peacock, is bred from blue peacocks. Its gorgeous white feathers are as beautiful as a wedding gown!
The Congo peacock comes from the Democratic Republic of Congo. It has a crown of white feathers on its head.
The golden peafowl is a species of green peafowl. During mating, its neck glows golden.
Seals live in the polar and subpolar regions around the ocean. They inhabit coastal areas and are therefore rarely seen in open water.
The cute little kinkajou is a nocturnal arboreal animal native to Central and South America. It differs from other small bear species in that it has a long, prehensile tail.
This lizard can reach up to 1.40 meters and weigh up to 5 kg. It is very impressive, especially when it stands up!
This Australian native has raised nostrils that allow it to breathe underwater. It only has to lift its head slightly and glide effortlessly beneath the surface.
The giant among Australian monitor lizards! It reaches up to 2 meters and has a uniquely patterned skin. It lives mainly on the ground and burrows at night.
Ferrets were also known to the ancient Greeks and Romans, when they were used to catch mice, rats, and wild rabbits.

The fat-tailed scorpion is one of the most venomous scorpion species in the world. It is native to the Sahara Desert and spends most of its day buried in the sand or hidden under rocks. It has a fairly aggressive nature and is responsible for several deaths each year.
The emperor scorpion, known in Latin as "Pandinus cavimanus", is a rather peaceful creature. Thanks to its gentle nature and unpretentiousness, it is very suitable for breeding.

Watch your step! The venom of the death-dealing scorpion is a deadly weapon. A sting from this scorpion can be fatal even to an adult. In nature, it is found in dry to semi-desert areas, where it hides in crevices and under rocks.
The Margay, or Long-tailed Ocelot, is an excellent tree climber and can hang from a branch on one leg. It lives in tropical and subtropical rainforests.
Everyone knows that storks carry babies. That's why they have long beaks - so they can bring newborns to young parents.
The emu is a flightless bird. Although it has several wings, its size and weight prevent it from flying.
Wildebeest live in the savannah and migrate annually, looking for new pastures after the rainy season.
The jackal is a type of canine. It has long legs and is well adapted for running long distances. This helps it in hunting.
These exotic Australian marsupials have stripes on their bodies, pointed snouts, and long sticky tongues. Their favorite food is termites.
Falcons have accompanied humans since time immemorial. Thanks to their sharp eyesight, they are often used in hunting.
Baby marmosets are active during the day. They jump from one branch to another. They drill holes in the bark of trees and suck their sap.
This small monkey, belonging to the marmoset family, has a long, bushy tail that is longer than its body. That's quite a mane!
A black-and-gray curled tail and striking white, bushy ears - that's how you'll recognize this marmoset as it swings from vine to vine in the Brazilian rainforest.
Mustangs are feral descendants of horses brought to the American continent by Spanish conquistadors. They are unpretentious and hardy animals.
The kiwi is the smallest species of flightless bird, lives in New Zealand and is also a national symbol. It is monogamous and can live with one partner for up to 10 years.
What's that flying around? The butterfly has always been associated with gentle beauty and freedom. It flies wherever it wants, from flower to flower.
Wet fur is no problem for otters. They have the thickest fur in the entire animal kingdom.
The green turtle stands out for its highly wavy shell, called the carapace. Each plate has a conspicuous yellow spot in the middle.
This species of turtle is characterized by the upward-curving, bifurcated spurs on its neck. Its age can be recognized by the distinctive growth rings on its shell.
Turtles usually live to be very old. Some have even lived to be 200 years old. This specimen still looks very well-preserved even at this old age.
Moles love to dig in gardens! They loosen the soil, but they also leave unsightly mounds on the lawn.
This cattle may look dangerous due to its horns, but the opposite is true.
This tall flightless bird once lived on the islands of Mauritius and Réunion. Unfortunately, it became extinct in the 17th century. However, we found a few eggs and were able to secretly raise some.
These large birds of prey feed on carrion and have a wingspan of up to 3 meters. Their characteristic feature is their featherless necks!
The green-crowned hummingbird can reach a length of up to 13 centimeters. Its flat tail is especially beautiful!
The Long-crested Hummingbird moves gracefully through life. The red crest on the male's head gives it a regal appearance!
This amazing creature has two long tails, at the ends of which are small iridescent blue discs - as if it were from another world!
This lizard is easily recognizable by its spiky scales on its body, legs, sides, and tail. It also looks like it has a beard on its head!
The pangolin has horny scales all over its body, and when it feels threatened, it curls up into a ball.
Mouflon are wild sheep and herds navigate by distinct landmarks in the landscape. They can survey the area from high places.
How fluffy! The Mangalica pig's fur is rough on the surface, but it's quite soft underneath. It's a very tame animal. Come to me, come. Cro, croc!
The Puli dog is a breed developed in Hungary and has been the best friend of local shepherds for centuries. Their wiry coat keeps them warm. Cute!
Quokkas are marsupials, a class of animals that includes kangaroos. They are also known as kangaroo rats. They hop on their own trails - hoppy hop!

The white-tailed ksukol is a rather fearsome-looking prosimian. Its outer coat is tipped with white, but its hands and feet are black. Another distinguishing feature: it is skinny and has spidery fingers.
As the pale moon city casts an eerie glow over your zoo, the werewolves come to life and howl eerily at the moon.
When they were discovered, turkeys were misidentified as a species of guinea fowl. They were brought to Central Europe from the New World.
Hairy marmots do best in cooler climates. They have few sweat glands and can easily overheat at temperatures around 20°C.
The common weasel is usually brown with white fur on its belly. Some species have fur that is as white as snow during the winter. At our zoo we call it the Arctic snow weasel.
Huskies are loyal companions of Siberian nomads who often travel on sleds. Together they make an unbeatable team!
The hoatzin's digestive system is similar to that of ruminants. Digestive bacteria break down food during digestion, causing its strong odor. This is why it is called the stink bird.
With its small body and thick fur, this cat looks like a brave teddy bear. Its fur protects it from the cold, especially when crossing the steppes of the Caucasus, Mongolia and Siberia.
A hairless cat? Does she like to cuddle like other cats? Of course! Sphynx cats love to cuddle and are incredibly lively and intelligent!

The desert fox - also known as the Arabian fox - roams the Sahara Desert and is common throughout Egypt. Like birds, lizards and rodents, this fox likes to climb date palm trees and feast on sweet dates. A mischievous creature!
The clouded leopard, named for the dark spots on its fur, is a very shy cat about which little is known.
The plumage of the scarlet ibis is stunning at first sight. When these red birds fly in a flock over a green meadow, it is a real spectacle.
The lovable pug is a cheerful and even-tempered companion. He has a tendency to be overweight, so a balanced diet is very important. Many people see him as a lap dog, but he can also be courageous.
The cheetah has exceptionally long legs and a slender body, making it look more like a greyhound than a cat. It also has a very small head.

That's a snort! The black-nosed kangaroo is known for its large, pear-shaped nose, which only males have. No one knows what it's for! It doesn't give them a better sense of smell, but scientists believe that the longer the nose, the more attractive the females are!
This domestic sheep is called the "Wallis Blacknose Sheep". It has a black head and black legs. Sometimes even the tails are black. It makes a nice color contrast with the white wool!
Dikdik? A strange name! This name is actually onomatopoeic and describes the sounds it makes when it warns others of predators. As an antelope, it is always on the alert!
The capybara is the largest rodent in the world, and its closest relatives are guinea pigs.
This colorful gecko is one of the largest of its kind, measuring up to 24 cm.
The spectacled bear is the last living of its kind. It is also the only bear species in South America and is known as the "Andean bear".
This nocturnal marsupial is often called the "eared rabbit" and is native to Australia. It's actually a cross between a rabbit and an anteater.
This colourful tropical bird is characterised by its sharp claws and helmet-like head. It is a flightless bird and can fly away quickly if threatened.
The largest and wildest South American dog got its name because of its distinctively patterned coat, which is more like a mane.
This huge snake offers free hugs! But make no mistake! He's not trying to be friendly! Although it's not venomous, it's not afraid to strangle its prey.
The wings of this warbler are white on the surface and marked with black vertical stripes. Very elegant!
The squirrel glider got its name because its behavior is similar to that of squirrels.
The warthog lives in the savannas and forests of sub-Saharan Africa, which is why it got this name.
The spotted hyena has relatively long and shaggy fur. The gray mane runs from the ear to the tail.
The Canada lynx silently steps on its soft paws. It prefers forested areas and snowy landscapes.
The kingfisher has a light turquoise stripe on its back that runs from top to bottom. With its white-colored neck and reddish belly, it has quite colorful plumage.
Cat, dog, ferret – it may seem like all three of these animal species are represented here. However, the cat-like ferret is neither a cat, nor a ferret, nor a dog. It’s a raccoon!
Praying mantises are very good at camouflaging themselves. They blend perfectly with the grassy surroundings and leaves, where they hunt their prey.
Silkie chickens come in a wide range of colors. The white Silkie chicken is the main attraction for families with children!
The lion-tailed macaque boasts a silver mane, similar to that of a lion. This monkey belongs to the genus Macaca.
The North American bison likes winter but is sensitive to moisture. No wonder, with its heavy fur coat.
Although the dingo is known as a wild dog in Australia, it actually does not bark. Instead, it howls and whimpers.
Pelicans are unique, especially because they have a large pouch under their beak.
Wombats may look cute, but they can be really big! They are among the largest burrowing mammals. They dig their burrows with their sharp claws, and they're perfect for cuddling!
The coati is a small carnivore from the bear family, also known as a 'coati.
The name 'cormorant' comes from Old French and means something like 'sea raven' or 'water raven.
This dragonfly got its name because of its emerald-colored eyes, which shine brightly in the sun.
The ocelot is also known as the 'dwarf leopard' and looks a bit like a domestic cat.
Oystercatchers live on the coast, where their lives are influenced by the tides. They search for food, such as mollusks and worms, on the shallows.
Because of its grunting sounds, it is also called the Tibetan grunting ox. It lives in Central Asia in extreme climatic conditions.
Dolphins are not only very playful, but also one of the most intelligent animals in the world!
In the rainforest, this magnificent bird displays its colorful feathers and courtship dance.
The gull belongs to a family of birds called long-wings, which are known for their loud cries. They are very sociable, but the noise can be deafening!
These cute snakes are only 60 to 80 cm long and have orange and black stripes.
This cute little animal eats its bite in no time - you better stay out of its way!
Unlike most other mongoose species, the striped mongoose is a social animal and lives in large colonies.
Crabs are found all over the world - in the sea, freshwater and on land! They are crustaceans with a strong exoskeleton, four pairs of legs and one pair of claws.
Kashmiri goats have very fluffy hair that makes wonderful knitting wool. Just watch out for the horns!
They can occasionally be spotted during the dark winter months: calons that fly out of the darkness are like werewolves. That's scary!
Whales are the largest mammals in the world. The giant sperm whale can be up to 20 metres long.
This single-humped camel comes from the Eastern Sahara and is well suited to the terrain there.
This seabird, which has distinctive blue swim bladders, is native to the Galapagos Islands and the Pacific coast of Central America.
The greater kudu is one of the largest antelope species and its shoulders can reach up to 160 cm in height.
This small marsupial is about the size of a house cat and can usually be found in Tasmania.
This giant squid can be up to 13 metres in length.
The blue marlin is a large Atlantic fish with a distinctive sword-like promontory and a long crested dorsal fin.
The colourful clownfish is native to the Indian and Pacific Oceans and is mainly known from movies.
They can be found all over the world and their tentacles sting intensely, but this humble jellyfish is the oldest multi-organ animal in the world.
The triggerfish belongs to the family Balistidae. When threatened, it inflates into a ball and sticks out its spines.
The Kraken is a giant mythological sea monster that drags ships to the bottom of the sea. But the Kraken in Water World is friendlier!
Seals live in the polar and subpolar regions of the ocean. They are found on the coast and are rarely seen in the water.
Pelicans are unique, especially because they have a large pouch under their beak.
The seagull belongs to the family of birds called long-winged birds, known for their loud cries. They are very social, but the sounds can be deafening!
Crabs are found all over the world - in the sea, freshwater and on land! They are crustaceans with a strong exoskeleton, four pairs of legs and one pair of claws.
This seabird, which has distinctive blue swimming webs, comes from the Galápagos and the Pacific coast of Central America.
Despite its appearance and upright swimming, the seahorse is a sea fish. There are 54 different species.
The highly social orca, known as the killer whale, hunts fish, rays, and smaller mammals, but is not dangerous to humans.
The cockatoo is a species of parrot known for its showy crest and curved beak. They are usually white, grey or black in colour.
Bonobo is also known as the dwarf chimpanzee. It has long legs, pink lips and long parted hair on its head.
Owls are nocturnal creatures, they hoot loudly and can turn their heads almost all the way around.
Manta rays are large aquatic creatures that live in tropical waters. They are an endangered species and can live up to 50 years.
The Malayan bear lives in tropical forests in Southeast Asia. It's easy to spot because of the yellow spot on its horseshoe-shaped neck.
The kakapo, also known as the owl parrot, is a flightless bird from New Zealand. It is critically endangered with a population of about 150 living individuals.
These small colourful birds are native to New Guinea and are known for their elaborate courtship rituals.
These small, colourful frogs are native to Central and South America. Not all are poisonous!
The highly social orca is known as the killer whale. It hunts fish, rays, and smaller mammals, but it is not dangerous to humans.
The highly social orca is known as the killer whale. It hunts fish, rays, and smaller mammals, but it is not dangerous to humans.
